data_flow_trace.original: Governance calls EpochConfig.setForNextEpoch(type(uint64).max) at src/runtime/EpochConfig.sol:99-113 — only == 0 is rejected; type(uint64).max passes and is stored in _pendingEpochIntervalMicros.
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: setForNextEpoch only rejects == 0; any non-zero uint64 including type(uint64).max is accepted and stored in _pendingEpochIntervalMicros, hasPendingConfig = true.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: requireAllowed(GOVERNANCE); _requireInitialized(); if (_epochIntervalMicros == 0) revert InvalidEpochInterval(). No upper bound / sanity check.

data_flow_trace.original: At the next natural epoch boundary, Blocker.onBlockStart() → Reconfiguration.checkAndStartTransition() → _canTransition() returns true → _doImmediateReconfigure() → _applyReconfiguration() at src/blocker/Reconfiguration.sol:258.
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: Blocker.onBlockStart calls IReconfiguration.checkAndStartTransition() (line 112, no try/catch). checkAndStartTransition evaluates _canTransition() at line 94 and, if DKG is Off, invokes _doImmediateReconfigure() -> _applyReconfiguration(). At the first boundary after the malicious set, the old (sane) epochIntervalMicros is still in effect, so the addition does not overflow and _canTransition() returns true normally.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: _transitionState != DkgInProgress; _canTransition() true using the OLD epochInterval (still sane). No try/catch around checkAndStartTransition() in Blocker.

data_flow_trace.original: _applyReconfiguration() line 267 calls EpochConfig(EPOCH_CONFIG).applyPendingConfig(): epochIntervalMicros is set to type(uint64).max. Line 293 sets lastReconfigurationTime = currentTime (~1.748e15 µs in 2025).
data_flow_trace.current_code_ref: src/blocker/Reconfiguration.sol:265-308 (applyPendingConfig call at 274; lastReconfigurationTime update at 293); src/runtime/EpochConfig.sol:122-138
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: _applyReconfiguration calls EpochConfig.applyPendingConfig() which unconditionally sets epochIntervalMicros = _pendingEpochIntervalMicros (no upper-bound validation at apply time either). Then lastReconfigurationTime is updated to current microsecond timestamp.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: applyPendingConfig has requireAllowed(RECONFIGURATION) and the hasPendingConfig early-return; no value sanity check.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Line numbers drift slightly vs. issue (apply call at 274, timestamp at 293 — within issue-cited range) but behavior identical.

data_flow_trace.original: On every subsequent block, Blocker.onBlockStart() → checkAndStartTransition() → _canTransition() at src/blocker/Reconfiguration.sol:208-212: `return currentTime >= lastReconfigurationTime + epochInterval` — ~1.748e15 + 18446744073709551615 overflows uint64 → Solidity ^0.8.30 checked-arithmetic Panic(0x11) revert.
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: _canTransition performs `lastReconfigurationTime + epochInterval` as unchecked-free uint64 addition under pragma ^0.8.30. Both operands are uint64; with epochInterval = type(uint64).max and any non-zero lastReconfigurationTime, the sum exceeds uint64 range and triggers Panic(0x11). There is no `unchecked { }` wrap, no pre-add bound check, and no saturation helper.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: none (pure arithmetic with Solidity 0.8 default checked math)
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Line range shifted from issue's 208-212 to 215-219, otherwise identical.

data_flow_trace.original: Blocker.onBlockStart() has NO try/catch around checkAndStartTransition() — Panic propagates to the VM block prologue → every block fails → permanent chain halt with no on-chain recovery.
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: onBlockStart invokes IReconfiguration(...).checkAndStartTransition() as a direct external call with no try/catch. Any revert (including Panic) propagates, reverting the block prologue. Blocker does have a ComponentUpdateFailed event declared but it is never used here; the call is bare.

reasoning_chain: Every link of the chain survives in the current code. Step 1: EpochConfig.setForNextEpoch (src/runtime/EpochConfig.sol:99-113) rejects only `_epochIntervalMicros == 0`; no MAX_EPOCH_INTERVAL_MICROS bound anywhere in src/. Step 3: EpochConfig.applyPendingConfig (lines 122-138) unconditionally copies the pending value. Step 4: _canTransition at src/blocker/Reconfiguration.sol:215-219 contains `return currentTime >= lastReconfigurationTime + epochInterval;` — bare uint64 addition under Solidity ^0.8.30 checked arithmetic, no unchecked block, no saturating helper, so lastReconfigurationTime + type(uint64).max panics. Step 5: Blocker.onBlockStart (src/blocker/Blocker.sol:112) calls checkAndStartTransition() directly with no try/catch, so the Panic propagates to the block prologue. The attack chain is unbroken, matching the issue description. The specific missing guard is any upper-bound check on _epochIntervalMicros in EpochConfig.setForNextEpoch/applyPendingConfig (or a saturating/unchecked wrap of the addition in _canTransition).

key_evidence.excerpt: function setForNextEpoch(uint64 _epochIntervalMicros) external { requireAllowed(SystemAddresses.GOVERNANCE); _requireInitialized(); if (_epochIntervalMicros == 0) { revert Errors.InvalidEpochInterval(); } _pendingEpochIntervalMicros = _epochIntervalMicros; hasPendingConfig = true; emit PendingEpochIntervalSet(_epochIntervalMicros); }
key_evidence.why_matters: Only lower bound (!= 0) is enforced; type(uint64).max is accepted into the pending slot.

key_evidence.excerpt: function _canTransition() internal view returns (bool) { uint64 currentTime = ITimestamp(SystemAddresses.TIMESTAMP).nowMicroseconds(); uint64 epochInterval = EpochConfig(SystemAddresses.EPOCH_CONFIG).epochIntervalMicros(); return currentTime >= lastReconfigurationTime + epochInterval; }
key_evidence.why_matters: Checked uint64 addition under pragma ^0.8.30; overflows to Panic(0x11) when epochInterval == type(uint64).max.
Bare external call with no try/catch — any revert from _canTransition aborts the entire block prologue.

key_evidence.excerpt: function applyPendingConfig() external { requireAllowed(SystemAddresses.RECONFIGURATION); _requireInitialized(); if (!hasPendingConfig) { return; } uint64 oldValue = epochIntervalMicros; epochIntervalMicros = _pendingEpochIntervalMicros; hasPendingConfig = false; _pendingEpochIntervalMicros = 0; emit EpochIntervalUpdated(oldValue, epochIntervalMicros); emit PendingEpochConfigCleared(); }
key_evidence.why_matters: Apply path also has no upper-bound validation; malicious pending value is latched as live config.

counterargument: Strongest defense: the attacker role must be GOVERNANCE (requireAllowed(SystemAddresses.GOVERNANCE) at line 102), and GOVERNANCE in this system is expected to be a multisig/DAO that already controls many existential parameters, so if it is compromised the chain is already lost (GIGO). One could argue this is not an independent vulnerability but a governance-risk category, and that the usual mitigation is off-chain governance hygiene and a guarded launch. Additionally, the economic/operational cost to trigger (an executed malicious proposal) is high, so real-world exploitability depends on governance delay/veto mechanisms outside this contract. That said, the issue is specifically about a contract-level reliability bug (a single miss-typed or malicious proposal bricks the chain with no on-chain recovery), which is the conventional rationale for adding input sanity bounds even on privileged setters — so the counterargument does not flip the verdict, only tempers exploit-likelihood.
